cards.grammarNote: Arabic doesn't simply add -s. Instead, many nouns change their internal vowel pattern to form the plural — these are called 'broken plurals.' The pattern must be memorized with each word.
كتاب → كتب. The long ā vowel disappears and the vowel pattern changes from kitāb to kutub. This is a broken plural — the shape of the word changes.
بيت (bayt) → بيوت (buyut). The vowel pattern shifts and a long u vowel is inserted.
رجل (rajul) → رجال (rijal). A long ā vowel is inserted. Many human nouns use this fa'al → fi'al pattern.
طفل (tifl) → أطفال (atfal). A prefix أ is added and vowels change — the أفعال pattern is one of the most common broken plural patterns.
صديق (sadiq) → أصدقاء (asdiqa'). Another أفعلاء pattern. Note the ء (hamza) at the end — common in broken plurals of this type.
Not all plurals are broken. Many words, especially loanwords and feminine nouns, take the 'sound' feminine plural by adding ات: سيارة → سيارات (cars), كلمة → كلمات (words).
كلمة (kalima) → كلمات. Add ات to the stem (drop the ة first): كلم + ات = كلمات. This is the sound feminine plural.
Masculine human nouns often take + ون (nominative) or + ين (accusative/genitive): مدرّس → مدرّسون (teachers). This is called the sound masculine plural.
مدرّس + ون = مدرّسون. Sound masculine plural — the word shape stays the same, only the suffix changes.
مدرّسة → مدرّسات. Drop the ة, add ات. Sound feminine plural.
